questions.question: Define the Waterfall Model and explain its fundamental characteristic that distinguishes it from iterative models. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Linear sequential flow through distinct phases
Non-overlapping phases with formal review gates
Each phase produces documentation as input to next
Progression only after approval of current phase deliverables

questions.question: Explain THREE advantages and THREE disadvantages of the Waterfall Model with specific justifications. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to understand, clear documentation, well-suited for stable requirements, easy to manage due to linear structure
Disadvantages: Late customer feedback, high risk of requirements changes, difficult to accommodate changes after phase completion, late defect discovery

questions.question: When is the Waterfall Model most appropriate to use? Provide at least FOUR specific project scenarios with justifications. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Projects with well-defined stable requirements
Regulatory/compliance-driven projects needing documentation
Small to medium projects with clear deliverables
Technology is well-understood and stable
Fixed-price contracts requiring predictable milestones

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'Phase Dependency' in Waterfall Model and how it creates risk for projects with uncertain requirements. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Each phase depends on deliverables of previous phase
Output of one phase becomes input to next
Changes in early phases cascade to all downstream phases
Cost of change increases exponentially as project progresses

questions.question: Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the context of Waterfall Model. Which activity receives more emphasis and why? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Verification: Are we building the product right? (conformance to specifications)
Validation: Are we building the right product? (meeting customer needs)
Waterfall emphasizes verification through phase reviews
Validation primarily occurs during Testing phase, late in lifecycle

questions.question: Explain the 'Narrowing Funnel' concept in Waterfall projects. How does it affect decision-making flexibility as the project progresses? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Options and flexibility decrease with each phase
Requirements phase offers maximum possibilities
Design commits to specific architecture and approach
Implementation locks in code decisions
Later changes require going back to earlier phases, increasing cost exponentially

questions.question: A mid-sized software company is considering Waterfall for their new e-commerce platform project. The client has provided initial requirements but expects to refine them based on market feedback during development. Additionally, the client wants regular demonstrations of working software. Evaluate whether Waterfall is appropriate for this project. If not, suggest an alternative model and justify your recommendation with at least FIVE points. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Waterfall is NOT appropriate because: (1) Requirements are not stable - client expects to refine based on feedback, (2) Client wants regular working software demonstrations, (3) E-commerce needs adaptability to market changes, (4) Late testing will delay market feedback, (5) High risk of scope changes in dynamic market
Recommended: Agile (Scrum or XP) or Incremental model

Justification: Allows iterative development with regular increments, Customer feedback incorporated throughout, Changes can be accommodated in subsequent sprints, Working software delivered early and frequently, Better risk management through early and continuous delivery

questions.question: Compare the Waterfall Model with the V-Model. Explain how the V-Model addresses Waterfall's weakness of late testing while maintaining similar structure. Include a diagram description and TWO advantages and TWO limitations of the V-Model. (10 marks)
keyPoints: V-Model extends Waterfall by mapping testing activities to each development phase
Unit Testing corresponds to Implementation, Integration Testing corresponds to System Design, etc.
Advantage 1: Testing begins earlier in lifecycle - defects discovered earlier
Advantage 2: Clear correspondence between development and testing activities improves quality
Limitation 1: Still rigid - doesn't handle requirements changes well
Limitation 2: Doesn't provide working software until late like Waterfall
The V-shape visualizes how left-side development phases relate to right-side testing phases

questions.question: A defense software project with fixed requirements and regulatory compliance has been using Waterfall. Midway through implementation, the client requests a major feature addition that will impact the system architecture. Using Waterfall principles, analyze: (a) What is the process for handling this change? (b) What will be the cost and schedule impact? (c) How could this situation have been prevented? (10 marks)
keyPoints: (a) Process: Submit Change Request → Impact Analysis → Approval from change control board → Return to Requirements phase → Redesign → Re-implement → Re-test
(b) Impact: Estimated 40-60% schedule delay, significant cost increase (change cost multiplier), downstream phases affected, may require additional testing cycles

(c) Prevention: Comprehensive requirements gathering initially, Prototyping before full development, Change control procedures with strict criteria, Consider iterative model if change likelihood is high, Include contingency in schedule/budget for changes
